html:
Pure rotation about the vertical axis:
u = ΩY(f + x2/f),  v = ΩYxy/f
⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: three horizontally-arranged, closely-spaced rectangular image panels (left, center, right), each with width greater than height to match textbook aspect ratio
dense, regular grid of vector field sample points over each panel (at least 18x9 arrows per panel), uniformly spaced
in each panel, a 2D vector field of crisp, sharp arrows showing image motion flow at each grid point
vector field computed for three preset focal lengths: f=1/3 (left), f=1 (center), f=3 (right)
under each panel: annotation or label indicating its focal length (e.g., 'f=1/3', 'f=1', 'f=3')
horizontal and vertical image axes (X and Y) lightly drawn for orientation
optional: faint bounding frames around each panel for clarity

interactions.label: Focal length f
interactions.teaches: Altering focal length f directly changes the form of the motion field induced by camera Y-rotation—small f causes diverging, perspective-like flows, large f yields nearly uniform lateral flows.

interactions.label: Rotation speed W_Y
interactions.teaches: Setting the rotation rate W_Y uniformly scales the length of all flow vectors, emphasizing the separation between velocity magnitude and flow direction pattern.

interactions.label: Vector density
interactions.teaches: Increasing vector density reveals fine spatial details in the motion field and clarifies divergence and parallelism patterns across the panels.

interactions.label: Show one panel
interactions.teaches: Toggles between viewing single focal length panels and comparing all three side-by-side, helping isolate or directly contrast the effects of f.

demo_steps.title: Divergent flow at short focal length
demo_steps.narration: Let's examine the left panel at f=1/3 with typical rotation speed. Notice how the arrows radiate outward and grow rapidly as you move away from the image center—flow direction and size both change with position. This is the signature of wide-angle perspective: apparent motion is highly non-uniform, mimicking a homography-induced flow.

demo_steps.title: Progression with increasing focal length
demo_steps.narration: Switch to the central panel (f=1). The arrows become more parallel at the center and less divergent toward the edges. While perspective effects are diminished, edge vectors still fan outward. This intermediate regime bridges the gap between strongly perspective and nearly translation-like motion.

demo_steps.title: Uniform, lateral flow at long focal length
demo_steps.narration: Now look at the right panel (f=3). Here, the arrows are almost all horizontal, equally long, and parallel—across the entire image. Increasing focal length suppresses the perspective divergence. The motion field now closely resembles pure lateral translation, regardless of position.

demo_steps.title: Motion field magnitude scales with rotation speed
demo_steps.narration: Increase the rotation rate W_Y while keeping focal length long. See how all arrows across the right panel grow in length together without changing their alignment or relative proportions. This reinforces that W_Y only affects magnitude, not flow pattern.

demo_steps.title: Compare all panels side by side
demo_steps.narration: Now let's view all three panels together. You can clearly see how the field transforms from divergent (left) to uniform (right) as focal length increases. This direct visual comparison makes the transition from perspective-dominated to translation-like motion unmistakable.

plan.camera_suggestion: Straight-on, orthographic front view with all panels horizontally arranged, filling most of the window; aspect ratio of each panel is about 2:1 width:height as in the textbook. Allow for zoom-in if single panel is selected.
plan.notes: Render each arrow as a crisp, anti-aliased line with sharp triangular head; set arrow thickness for clarity at high density. Compute flow vectors analytically: for Y-rotation W_Y and focal length f, flow at (x, y) is u = -f*W_Y + x*y*W_Y, v = y^2*W_Y (or simplified per the figure's linear model: in large f limit, horizontal, in small f, divergent). Use normalized panel coordinates matching textbook for consistency. Maintain equal arrow scaling within each panel; regulate maximum arrow length for clarity. Synchronize slider-driven updates across all rendered panels. Ensure labels for f below panels are crisp and match slider value if used interactively.

plan.contextChunk:
\end{bmatrix}
W_Y 
\end{aligned}$$ 

In this case, the focal length $f$ will have a strong
effect on the appearance of the motion field. For very large $f$, we can
approximate the 2D motion field by $\dot{x} \approx f W_Y$ and
$\dot{y} \approx 0$. The resulting motion field is approximately
constant across the entire image and looks like lateral translation
motion. For very small $f$, the motion field will be similar to the one
produced by a homography and it will be very different to a lateral
camera motion. The flows shown in @fig-motion_wy_f03_f1_f3 correspond
to $f=1/3$, $f=1$, and $f=3$.

![Motion flows corresponding to a rotation around the 
$Y$-axis. (left) $f = 1/3$. (middle) $f = 1$. (right) $f = 3$.](figures/optical_flow/camera_motion_field_rotation_three_f.png){width="100%" #fig-motion_wy_f03_f1_f3}

Camera rotation around the $Y$-axis does not inform about the scene
structure, but it informs about the camera parameters. The magnitude of
$W_Y$ only affects the scaling of the motion vectors, but it does not
change their orientation.

In the case of camera rotation, for a large angle (or a large
$\Delta t$), the relationship between the image at time $t$ and the
image at time $t+\Delta t$ is a homography.

### Motion under Varying Focal Length

Before moving into motion estimation, let's consider one final scenario:
a static camera observing a static scene, but the focal length changes
over time. What will the motion field be? In this setting, despite that

elements: three rectangular image planes (left, center, right) arranged side by side
dense 2D grid of points in each image plane
vector field (arrow at each grid point) representing apparent motion induced by rotation about the Y-axis
arrow sizes and orientations computed for three different focal lengths: f=1/3 (left), f=1 (center), f=3 (right)
axis labels or annotations showing f values beneath each rectangle
rectangular frames for each image region

interactions.label: Focal length f
interactions.teaches: Adjusting the focal length f changes the geometry of the induced motion field for pure camera Y-rotation—small f creates a perspective flow, large f approximates a lateral flow.

interactions.label: Rotation speed W_Y
interactions.teaches: Changing the angular velocity W_Y scales the length of the flow vectors proportionally, but does not affect their orientation pattern.

demo_steps.title: Small focal length: divergent flow
demo_steps.narration: With a small focal length (f = 0.33), the motion field for Y-rotation looks highly non-uniform. The flow vectors diverge outwards, especially near the edges of the image, resembling the motion under a homography. This shows strong perspective effects.

demo_steps.title: Moderate focal length: mixed behavior
demo_steps.narration: At a moderate focal length (f = 1), the vectors are less divergent. The central part of the image has nearly horizontal, parallel flows, while the outer regions still show some spreading. We are halfway between the homography-like and translation-like cases.

demo_steps.title: Large focal length: nearly lateral flow
demo_steps.narration: Set the focal length to f = 3. Now the flow vectors become almost perfectly horizontal and uniform across the whole image. The pattern is nearly identical to pure lateral image translation. Perspective effects become negligible at high f.

demo_steps.title: Scaling the rotation speed
demo_steps.narration: Increase the rotation speed W_Y. All flow vectors lengthen proportionally, but their pattern stays the same. W_Y controls only the overall motion magnitude, not the shape of the field.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Orthogonal front view filling the window with all three image planes visible side by side. Enough zoom to clearly see the motion vectors inside each rectangle.
interactionPlan.notes: For each focal length value, recompute the flow field using the formula for image velocity under pure Y-rotation with adjustable W_Y and f. Ensure arrow directions/lengths update in real-time with sliders. Optionally allow dragging across the image to inspect vector values at specific points. Annotations for each f value should update dynamically if the slider is used instead of fixed subplots.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Generated vector fields do not match the original (source varies linearly along both dimensions, generated field equations differ).
Arrow density and uniformity in the generated version are too coarse compared to the reference, which has a denser vector field.
Arrow scaling and directions are not visually consistent with source—original has correct divergence/convergence, generated arrows behave differently.
Generated boxes are more square and vertically compressed, whereas original panels are more rectangular (wider).
Generated arrowheads and shafts are less crisp/defined compared to the smooth, sharp arrows in the source.
evaluation.notes: Interactivity and labeling are strong, but the underlying vector field and proportions are misconstrued, leading to low faithfulness and concept accuracy.
action_items: Revise the vector field to accurately reflect the original (use linear field for each panel matching textbook's divergence and direction).
Increase the density and improve the clarity of the arrows—consider rendering sharper arrows and regulating size legibly.
Adjust panel aspect ratios and spacing to match the original's more horizontal rectangles.
Ensure the arrows demonstrate linear divergence, uniform flow, and increasing flow correctly as in the textbook source.
Enhance arrow drawing for smoother lines and clearer heads to match the crispness of the original.

feedback.rationale: The critic highlights a fundamental misunderstanding of the vector field concept and panel proportions, indicating that the interaction plan and conceptual decomposition require correction before refining implementation.
action_items: Revise the vector field to accurately reflect the original (use linear field for each panel matching textbook's divergence and direction).

Adjust panel aspect ratios and spacing to match the original's more horizontal rectangles.
Ensure the arrows demonstrate linear divergence, uniform flow, and increasing flow correctly as in the textbook source.
Enhance arrow drawing for smoother lines and clearer heads to match the crispness of the original.

discrepancies: UI controls and descriptive text overlap the leftmost panel, adding clutter not present in the source.
Slider and button controls are visible, introducing extra elements not in the original image.
Arrow density and alignment in the generated panels match well, but slightly more crowded at edges.
Frame lines and extra edge elements (perspective hints?) are slightly heavier or more prominent than in the source.
Panel aspect ratios and positions are close but not pixel-perfect.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive reproduction with strong fidelity; minor visual clutter and small layout deviations but concept and labeling are robust.
action_items: Move UI controls and descriptive text away from the drawing area to eliminate overlap/clutter in the visual region.
Slightly refine panel layout and proportions for pixel-perfect alignment with the reference.
Reduce frame stroke weight and ensure extra perspective/edge hints do not distract from the clean panel borders as in the reference.
Fine-tune arrow outset at the panel edges to better match density without crowding.

feedback.rationale: The issues are related to layout, visual clutter, and minor rendering differences, indicating the plan is correct but implementation needs refinement.

attempts.html:
Pure rotation about the vertical axis:
u = ΩY(f + x2/f),  v = ΩYxy/f
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: UI controls and instructional box visually overlap the leftmost panel's arrows and label, creating clutter not present in the source figure.
Arrow densities, arrowhead sharpness, and uniformity/proportionality differ somewhat from the source; the source figure has slightly different vector field density and size distribution.
Panel frames and outside 'extrusions' are visually fainter than in the original, particularly for the rightmost 'spine' rectangles.
Math label placement under each panel is not as centered/balanced below the rectangles as in the source figure.
Color and typeface are generally correct, but some label font sizes and arrow thicknesses can still be adjusted for closer fidelity.
evaluation.notes: Very strong match in geometry, math, and function; improves on the textbook via meaningful controls, though clutter and exact arrow/label visual match could be refined.
action_items: Move UI controls and instructions away from overlapping the leftmost panel and its label to reduce clutter.
Refine arrow head shape and arrow density to precisely match the proportions in the source image.
Center and reposition panel labels for better balance under the panels, paying attention to exact vertical and horizontal placement.
Darken or thicken the side rectangles/extrusions on the right of each panel so they match the boldness in the textbook.
Fine-tune label font sizes and arrow thickness for closer typographic fidelity.

feedback.rationale: All action items pertain to layout, styling, and fine-tuning the visual implementation rather than conceptual or structural issues in the plan.
